channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: ‘Trumpflation’ warning: mortgage bills could rise by £3,000 annually – Moneyfacts
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage costs could rise significantly in the coming months, with annual repayments potentially increasing by thousands of pounds, according to recent analysis by Moneyfacts. The findings highlight how geopolitical tensions, energy price pressures, and inflation could impact borrowing costs for homeowners. Historical data spanning over three decades suggests that mortgage rates typically sit 1.5% to 1.75% above the Bank of England base rate, meaning any changes in monetary policy can quickly affect mortgage repayments.

Potential Scenarios for Inflation and Mortgage Rates

Moneyfacts has outlined three possible scenarios based on current economic uncertainties. In the worst-case scenario, referred to as "Trumpflation," sustained high oil prices above $120 and inflation peaking at 6.2% could push the base rate to 5.25%. This would result in average mortgage rates rising to around 6.75%. For a typical £250,000 repayment mortgage over 25 years, monthly repayments could increase to £1,727, or £20,724 annually. This represents an annual rise of approximately £3,380 compared to pre-conflict levels.

The most optimistic scenario, Scenario A, assumes energy prices ease quickly, with inflation peaking at 3.6% this year before falling below 3% next autumn. In this case, mortgage rates could edge down sooner, potentially keeping repayments closer to pre-conflict levels. The central case, Scenario B, envisions energy prices falling more slowly, with inflation reaching 3.7% and staying elevated for longer. This path, currently viewed as the most likely, suggests mortgage rates could stabilise around current levels with only modest upward pressure.

What This Means for Borrowers

For homeowners, these scenarios underscore the importance of staying informed about potential changes in the economic landscape. Borrowers nearing the end of their fixed-rate mortgage term might consider reviewing their options, particularly if they are concerned about rising interest rates. Those on variable-rate mortgages could also face higher repayments if the base rate increases, making it worth exploring whether switching to a fixed-rate deal could provide greater stability.

Whilst the worst-case scenario paints a concerning picture, it’s worth noting that these projections are based on hypothetical conditions. Economic forecasts can change rapidly, and external factors such as government intervention or shifts in global markets could influence outcomes. However, being prepared for potential increases in mortgage costs could help homeowners manage their finances more effectively.

In conclusion, whilst the full impact of inflation and geopolitical tensions on mortgage rates remains uncertain, homeowners may wish to proactively assess their borrowing arrangements. Keeping an eye on market developments and seeking independent financial advice could help ensure they are well-positioned to navigate any changes in the months ahead.

item.title: BTR data shows strong appetite but slow starts
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research reveals a notable shift in build-to-rent (BTR) investment patterns, with over three-fifths of sector capital now flowing into operational developments rather than new projects. The latest quarterly analysis from Knight Frank highlights how investors are increasingly favouring income-generating assets over ground-up construction in the current economic climate.

Investors favour income-producing BTR assets

The Q1 2026 market update shows operational BTR properties accounted for 61% of total sector investment, suggesting a cautious approach from institutional backers. This preference for stabilised assets with existing tenant income streams may reflect concerns about construction costs and longer development timelines in the current market.

Several factors could be driving this trend:

  • Higher financing costs making development projects less attractive
  • Strong tenant demand supporting valuations of existing portfolios
  • Investors seeking quicker returns amid economic uncertainty

Challenges for new BTR developments

Whilst appetite for the sector remains robust overall, the data indicates slower momentum for new build-to-rent projects. This could potentially create supply challenges in the medium term if the trend continues, particularly in high-demand urban areas where BTR has become an important part of the rental market.

Industry observers suggest the current investment pattern might lead to:

  • Reduced pipeline of new BTR properties coming to market
  • Potential rental growth in existing operational assets
  • Increased competition among tenants for quality rental homes

The BTR sector continues to evolve, with investors carefully balancing risk and reward in their portfolio strategies. As the market adjusts to current economic conditions, both developers and renters may need to adapt to these shifting investment priorities.

item.title: Buy-to-let market remains viable investment as RRA brings certainty, claims mortgage broker
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK buy-to-let market continues to attract investors, with many viewing it as a stable and viable opportunity despite recent regulatory changes. The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) has brought greater transparency and certainty for landlords, according to industry experts. Whilst some may have anticipated a downturn in property investment activity, confidence remains high amongst those looking to expand or enter the buy-to-let sector.

Impact of the Renters’ Rights Act

The Renters’ Rights Act, designed to enhance tenant protections, has introduced clearer guidelines for landlords. This legislation aims to balance the interests of both tenants and property investors, ensuring fair practices across the rental market. For landlords, the RRA provides a more structured framework, reducing ambiguity and fostering a sense of predictability. This clarity has been welcomed by many investors, who now have a better understanding of their responsibilities and rights under the new rules.

Confidence in the Buy-to-Let Market

Despite concerns that the RRA might deter potential investors, the buy-to-let market has shown resilience. Property investment activity remains robust, with landlords continuing to see opportunities for long-term returns. The stability of the rental market, coupled with historically low mortgage rates in recent years, has contributed to sustained interest in buy-to-let properties. For those considering this investment route, it could be worth evaluating the potential benefits and risks in light of the current regulatory landscape.

Landlords and prospective investors might also consider seeking professional advice to navigate the complexities of the market. Mortgage brokers and financial advisers can provide tailored insights, helping individuals make informed decisions that align with their financial goals.

As the buy-to-let market evolves, staying informed about legislative changes and market trends remains crucial. With the right approach, property investment can continue to be a viable option for those looking to diversify their portfolios.

item.title: Investment group completes first acquisition as part of wider growth strategy
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

NLG Investment Group Ltd has announced its first acquisition, bringing Warner Property Services into its portfolio. The Kent-based property inventory business will now operate alongside No Letting Go and Kaptur Software, creating a comprehensive offering for letting agents, build-to-rent operators and institutional landlords. This move marks a significant step in NLG Investment Group’s strategy to expand its footprint in the property reporting and compliance sector.

A Combined Approach to Property Reporting

The integration of Warner Property Services, No Letting Go and Kaptur Software aims to streamline property reporting and inventory services across the UK. Warner Property Services brings regional expertise and established client relationships, while No Letting Go provides a national network for property reporting. Kaptur Software will enhance the process by offering integrated reporting solutions, from instruction to compliance documentation and data management.

This combined approach reflects the growing demand for standardised reporting and compliance documentation in the lettings sector. By merging regional strengths with national reach and advanced technology, NLG Investment Group hopes to offer a consistent, high-quality service that meets the evolving needs of the industry.

Industry Insights and Future Plans

Nick Lyons, CEO of NLG Investment Group, highlighted the importance of this acquisition in shaping the future of property compliance services. “Bringing together strong regional expertise, a national delivery network and market-leading reporting technology allows us to build a nationwide solution that aligns with industry trends,” he said. “In these changing times, the ability to provide reliable evidence and data is crucial, and this partnership positions us to support our clients effectively.”

As part of the integration, Mark Weller of Warner Property Services will join the head office team at No Letting Go, overseeing sales in the lettings and property management market. Warner Property Services will continue to operate as a separate brand within NLG Investment Group, maintaining its existing client relationships while benefiting from the group’s broader resources.

What This Means for the Lettings Sector

This acquisition forms part of NLG Investment Group’s wider strategy to expand its property reporting and inventory services across the lettings sector. For letting agents, build-to-rent operators and institutional landlords, this could mean access to more efficient and standardised compliance solutions. The integration of regional expertise, national coverage and advanced technology aims to simplify processes and improve the quality of reporting.

As the lettings industry continues to evolve, partnerships like this could play a key role in meeting the growing demand for transparency and compliance. Borrowers and property managers may wish to keep an eye on how these developments impact the services available to them.

NLG Investment Group’s acquisition of Warner Property Services signals a commitment to innovation and growth in the property reporting sector. By combining regional strengths with national reach and cutting-edge technology, the group aims to set a new standard for property compliance services in the UK.

item.title: Rightmove launches new equity tracker
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property investors and homeowners seeking to gauge their available equity for future purchases might find interest in Rightmove's newly launched Equity Tracker tool. This innovation could help individuals assess their current property value against outstanding mortgage balances, potentially aiding financial planning for those considering moving home or expanding their property portfolio.

How the Equity Tracker works

The Equity Tracker functionality allows users to input their property details and mortgage information to receive an estimate of their available equity. The tool reportedly combines Rightmove's property valuation data with mortgage balance calculations to provide a comprehensive overview. Homeowners may wish to note that:

  • The tracker provides indicative figures rather than guaranteed valuations
  • Results could serve as a starting point for more detailed financial discussions
  • Regular updates might help monitor equity changes over time

Potential benefits for property owners

For those contemplating their next property move, understanding available equity could prove valuable when:

  • Considering upsizing or downsizing their home
  • Exploring buy-to-let investment opportunities
  • Assessing remortgage options to release capital

While the tool offers convenience, borrowers might consider consulting a mortgage adviser for personalised guidance on how to potentially utilise any identified equity. The tracker's automated nature means it cannot account for individual circumstances or future market fluctuations that might affect property values.

As property markets continue to evolve, tools like Rightmove's Equity Tracker may become increasingly useful for homeowners monitoring their financial position. However, it remains important to remember that online valuations should ideally be supplemented with professional advice when making significant financial decisions.

item.title: Cash Buyers vs Mortgage Buyers Which Is Better
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

When purchasing property in the UK, buyers typically fall into two camps: those paying with cash and those requiring a mortgage. Each approach comes with distinct advantages and considerations that can influence both the buying process and negotiation power. Understanding these differences may help prospective buyers determine which route aligns best with their circumstances.

The advantages of cash purchases

Cash buyers often enjoy several benefits in property transactions. Without mortgage applications to process, purchases can complete significantly faster – sometimes in a matter of weeks rather than months. This speed makes cash offers particularly attractive to sellers, potentially giving buyers stronger negotiating power on price.

Other advantages include:

  • No mortgage fees or interest payments
  • Greater certainty of completion (no risk of mortgage refusal)
  • Potential to secure discounts from motivated sellers
  • Ability to purchase properties that might not meet mortgage valuation criteria

Why mortgage buyers shouldn't be discounted

While cash purchases have clear benefits, mortgaged buyers still represent the majority of UK property transactions. Modern mortgage products offer flexibility that cash buyers might lack, particularly when it comes to preserving liquidity. Borrowers might consider that:

  • Mortgage rates, whilst higher than in recent years, remain historically competitive
  • Keeping capital invested elsewhere could potentially yield higher returns
  • Fixed-rate deals provide payment certainty for several years
  • Government schemes like Help to Buy can assist first-time buyers

For homeowners needing to move quickly despite requiring a mortgage, products like porting an existing mortgage or securing an agreement in principle beforehand could help strengthen their position.

Ultimately, the "better" option depends entirely on individual financial circumstances and property goals. Both cash and mortgage purchases have valid places in the property market, each serving different needs and strategies. Prospective buyers may wish to consult a mortgage adviser to explore all available options before committing to a purchase approach.

item.title: Six ways the Renters Rights Act will change renting: Part 6, Database and Ombudsman
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters Rights Act is set to introduce significant changes to the rental market in the UK, with two key developments expected to roll out later this year: the introduction of a Landlords Database and a new Landlord Ombudsman scheme. These changes aim to bring greater transparency and accountability to the sector, benefiting both tenants and landlords alike.

The Landlords Database

One of the most anticipated changes is the launch of the Landlords Database, which is expected to become operational in the near future. Currently, there is no centralised system for tracking landlords, their properties, or their locations, making it challenging for authorities to enforce regulations effectively. This database will address this gap by creating a comprehensive record of landlords and their rental properties.

For local authorities, the database will streamline enforcement efforts and assist in identifying landlords who may not be complying with regulations. It could also prove valuable for the First Tier Tribunal, particularly when reviewing rent increases under Section 13, as access to rent records may help determine fair market rates. Additionally, whilst not explicitly stated, the database may aid HMRC in identifying landlords who have failed to declare rental income accurately.

Tenants are also likely to benefit, as the database could provide access to information about prospective landlords and their properties. However, it remains unclear what details will be publicly available and which will be restricted to authorised bodies. Landlords, on the other hand, may have concerns about additional costs, especially those already paying for HMO or selective licensing. The government has clarified that the database will complement, rather than replace, existing licensing schemes and may also serve as a platform to inform landlords about new legislation and provide guidance.

The Landlord Ombudsman Scheme

Another significant change is the introduction of a Landlord Ombudsman scheme, which has been long overdue. Since 2014, letting agents have been required to join a Property Redress Scheme, granting tenants recourse to an ombudsman if disputes arise. However, tenants renting directly from landlords have not had the same access to such services, creating an imbalance in tenant rights.

The new ombudsman scheme aims to address this disparity by offering tenants a formal avenue to resolve disputes with their landlords. This could include issues such as maintenance delays, deposit disputes, or breaches of tenancy agreements. For landlords, the scheme may encourage better compliance with regulations and foster more positive landlord-tenant relationships.

These upcoming changes under the Renters Rights Act mark a significant step forward in improving the rental market. By enhancing transparency and accountability, the Landlords Database and Landlord Ombudsman scheme could create a fairer and more efficient system for all parties involved.

item.title: Fines risk highlighted as data points to rollout gap for Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data suggests many landlords in England may be unprepared for upcoming Renters’ Rights Act requirements, with official compliance documents seeing limited uptake ahead of the 2026 deadline. Freedom of Information figures reveal the mandatory information sheet for tenants was downloaded just 153,000 times in its first month of availability – a small fraction of the estimated 2.3 million private landlords affected.

Compliance gap raises enforcement concerns

The Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government figures show 189,000 visits to the relevant GOV.UK page, indicating many landlords viewed the guidance without downloading the document. Whilst some compliance work may be handled by letting agents, the numbers suggest a significant portion of landlords haven’t yet engaged with the new requirements.

From 31 May 2026, landlords failing to provide tenants with the prescribed information could face fines of up to £7,000 per tenancy. The document outlines key tenant rights under the legislation, including protections against unfair evictions and rent increases.

Industry voices highlight implementation challenges

Property experts note the figures reveal uneven readiness across the private rental sector. Logan Ransley of Landlord Studio commented: “The findings show a rollout challenge – engagement levels appear low compared to the sector’s size. Landlords operate very differently; some have compliance systems in place whilst others rely on manual processes.”

The data suggests landlords may be approaching the deadline in different ways:

  • Some appear to be acting early to understand requirements
  • Others may be delaying whilst they assess operational changes needed
  • Portfolio landlords might be waiting to implement changes across multiple properties

Ransley added: “Enforcement deadlines often highlight these differences. It’s not necessarily about unwillingness to comply, but varying starting points in terms of systems and processes.”

With over two years remaining until the deadline, landlords might consider reviewing their compliance procedures. As well as providing the information sheet, they’ll need to maintain evidence of having done so for each tenancy. Letting agents managing properties on behalf of landlords may wish to confirm their approach to meeting these new obligations.

Letting agents managing properties on behalf of landlords may wish to confirm their approach to meeting these new obligations.

item.title: Fire your worst landlord: The counter-intuitive path to a more profitable portfolio
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Managing a property portfolio can be a rewarding venture, but some landlords may find that certain tenants or properties consume disproportionate amounts of time and resources. Whilst it might seem counterintuitive, letting go of problematic elements in your portfolio could lead to greater profitability and peace of mind in the long run.

The Pareto Principle in Property Management

In property management, the Pareto Principle—often referred to as the 80/20 rule—can be particularly striking. Approximately 20% of tenants or properties might generate 80% of the issues, whilst contributing only a small fraction of the overall revenue. For instance, landlords might spend hours dealing with minor complaints or justifying maintenance decisions, time that could be better spent on expanding their portfolio or improving their business operations.

Consider the financial implications: if a landlord spends four hours a week managing a property that earns £75 a month, they are effectively earning £18.75 per hour. This is before accounting for stress, additional costs, and the impact on other tenants who value a well-managed property. In essence, retaining problematic tenants or properties could be costing landlords more than they realise.

The Hidden Costs of Difficult Tenants

Beyond the immediate financial impact, keeping troublesome tenants or properties can have broader consequences for a landlord’s business. One significant cost is the lost opportunity to focus on growth. Every hour spent dealing with minor issues is an hour not spent on strategic planning, marketing, or improving tenant relationships. This can hinder a landlord’s ability to position themselves as a premium property manager in their area.

Additionally, difficult tenants can affect team morale. Staff members may become disheartened if they see their efforts being undervalued or if they are expected to compromise professional standards to accommodate unreasonable demands. In an industry where staff retention is already challenging, maintaining a positive and professional work environment is crucial.

Reputation is another key consideration. Properties associated with frequent complaints or regulatory issues can damage a landlord’s brand. If a tenant refuses to address necessary repairs and this leads to ongoing problems, it reflects poorly on the landlord’s management capabilities. Over time, this can deter potential tenants and partners, further impacting profitability.

Strategies for a Healthier Portfolio

Landlords might consider reviewing their portfolio regularly to identify properties or tenants that are causing disproportionate stress or financial strain. Whilst it can be difficult to let go of a source of income, the long-term benefits of a streamlined, well-managed portfolio could outweigh the short-term loss.

Focusing on high-quality tenants and properties can lead to more stable revenue and fewer unexpected issues. Additionally, investing in tenant screening processes and property maintenance can help prevent problems before they arise, allowing landlords to concentrate on growing their business.

Ultimately, managing a property portfolio is about balancing profitability with peace of mind. By addressing the hidden costs of difficult tenants and properties, landlords can create a more sustainable and rewarding business model.

In conclusion, whilst it might feel uncomfortable to part ways with certain tenants or properties, doing so could pave the way for a more profitable and manageable portfolio. Regular reviews and strategic decisions can help landlords focus on what truly matters—building a successful and stress-free property business.

item.title: Is the telephone dying in conveyancing – and is it slowing down your sales?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

In the fast-paced world of property transactions, communication methods are evolving rapidly. Whilst conveyancing has traditionally relied on phone calls to keep deals moving, digital tools such as email, messaging platforms and online portals are now dominating the landscape. However, this shift towards digital communication might be introducing unexpected delays into the process, raising concerns for estate agents and conveyancers alike.

The Rise of Digital Communication

Email and digital platforms have undeniably transformed the conveyancing process. They offer efficiency, scalability and a clear audit trail, which are essential in today’s high-volume environment. Case management systems integrate seamlessly with these tools, making it easier to handle large workloads. Yet, despite their advantages, digital methods are not without their drawbacks.

One conveyancer recently noted that whilst email has become the most prevalent method, it isn’t always the most effective. Simple queries that could be resolved in minutes over the phone can often lead to lengthy email chains, stretching over days. This can create friction, duplication and delays, particularly when dealing with complex or time-sensitive issues. For estate agents working to keep transactions on track, these delays can be frustrating and potentially costly.

The Limitations of Written Communication

Conveyancing is rarely straightforward. It involves legal terminology, multiple parties and often significant emotional pressure for buyers and sellers. In this context, written communication can sometimes fall short. Emails lack tone and context, which can lead to misinterpretation and slower progress. For agents, this can mean the difference between a transaction progressing smoothly or stalling at a critical moment.

Additionally, younger clients are increasingly reluctant to use the phone. Research suggests that around 23% of 18–34-year-olds hesitate to answer calls, preferring digital-first communication options. Whilst firms are adapting to these preferences, this shift creates a tension between offering a modern, client-friendly approach and ensuring transactions move efficiently.

Why the Phone Still Matters

Despite the growing reliance on digital tools, many conveyancers agree that the telephone remains an essential part of the process. It offers immediate clarification of issues, real-time problem solving and the ability to convey tone and urgency more effectively than written communication. For complex or time-sensitive matters, a quick phone call can often resolve issues far more efficiently than a string of emails.

Balancing the convenience of digital communication with the effectiveness of phone calls is key. Estate agents and conveyancers might consider adopting a hybrid approach, using digital tools for routine updates and reserving phone calls for urgent or complex matters. This could help streamline the process whilst ensuring critical issues are addressed promptly.

As the conveyancing landscape continues to evolve, finding the right balance between digital and traditional communication methods will be crucial. Whilst technology offers many benefits, recognising the value of the phone in certain situations could help keep transactions moving smoothly and reduce delays.

item.title: Understanding Smarter Financing Choices in a Changing Property Market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK property market continues to shift amidst changing economic conditions, fluctuating interest rates and evolving buyer behaviour. For those considering property purchases, remortgaging or investment opportunities, adapting financing strategies to current market realities could prove crucial. Careful planning may help borrowers secure favourable terms whilst maintaining financial stability for the years ahead.

Navigating Mortgage Options in Uncertain Times

With the Bank of England base rate influencing lender pricing, homeowners may wish to review their mortgage arrangements regularly. Fixed-rate deals currently dominate the market, offering payment certainty during periods of economic volatility. However, tracker or variable rate mortgages might suit those anticipating future rate drops.

Key considerations for borrowers include:

  • Comparing mortgage terms beyond just interest rates, including fees and flexibility
  • Assessing personal circumstances and risk tolerance when choosing between fixed and variable products
  • Monitoring equity positions which could unlock better loan-to-value tiers

Strategic Approaches for Different Property Goals

First-time buyers might explore government schemes like Help to Buy or Shared Ownership whilst interest rates remain elevated. Those trading up could benefit from porting existing mortgages to avoid early repayment charges. Property investors may wish to reassess portfolio financing structures, particularly with recent tax changes affecting buy-to-let profitability.

For remortgaging homeowners, it could be worth:

  • Reviewing deals three to six months before current terms expire
  • Considering overpayment options if financially viable
  • Evaluating whether to shorten or extend mortgage terms based on current affordability

As market conditions evolve, staying informed and seeking professional mortgage advice may help borrowers make confident financing decisions aligned with their property ambitions.

item.title: Are you up to speed with the Renters’ Rights Act?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

With the Renters’ Rights Act now coming into force, letting agents across England and Wales are seeking clarity on how the legislation affects their day-to-day operations. Rightmove has responded to this demand by hosting a specialist webinar today, offering professionals direct access to industry experts during a live Q&A session.

Understanding the legislative changes

The 10am webinar, delivered in partnership with Training For Professionals (TFP), aims to help agents navigate what Rightmove describes as "one of the biggest changes the lettings industry has seen in a generation". Over 1,000 professionals have already registered for the event, which covers practical implications of the new legislation across various tenancy scenarios.

Participants will have the opportunity to submit questions to David d’Orton-Gibson, TFP's managing director, whose background includes running a lettings agency and training housing professionals. The session forms part of Rightmove's wider support package, which includes 12 downloadable guides addressing specific areas such as student tenancies, pet policies and rent adjustments.

Tools for adapting to new requirements

Alongside the educational content, Rightmove is promoting its updated Tenancy Manager tool, now compliant with the Renters’ Rights Act. Webinar attendees will be offered a free trial of the software, which could help agents streamline their compliance processes as the new rules take effect.

For time-pressed professionals, the session represents a concentrated opportunity to gain practical insights. Jason Charles, Rightmove's head of education, emphasised the importance of agents feeling confident about implementing the changes: "This live Q&A gives agents the chance to get clarity on the questions they're facing right now, straight from experts who understand the realities of working in the sector."

With tenant expectations evolving alongside regulatory requirements, the webinar may prove valuable for agents looking to maintain compliance while delivering quality service. Those interested can still register through Rightmove's dedicated support hub, where additional resources remain available after the live event concludes.

item.title: Global uncertainty hits UK housing demand
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent research suggests that global economic uncertainty and rising living costs are influencing housing decisions across the UK. With concerns about interest rates and affordability growing, many homeowners appear to be adjusting their financial strategies, from increasing mortgage payments to delaying property moves.

Changing mortgage behaviours emerge

Barclays' latest Property Insights report indicates a notable shift in how borrowers are approaching their mortgages. Approximately 27% of homeowners surveyed reported making overpayments on their home loans, potentially to reduce future exposure to interest rate rises. Meanwhile, remortgaging activity has seen a 9% year-on-year increase, with 20% of those securing new deals citing a desire to lock in rates quickly amidst market volatility.

Industry experts note that much of this remortgaging activity likely relates to borrowers reaching the end of five-year fixed-rate agreements taken out in 2021, rather than being solely driven by recent geopolitical tensions. Nevertheless, 17% of UK adults reported that international conflicts have directly impacted their housing plans.

Moving plans face multiple hurdles

The research highlights several barriers preventing homeowners from moving properties. Economic uncertainty was cited by 29% of respondents as a potential reason to delay or cancel a move. Other significant obstacles include:

  • Moving costs (28%)
  • Stamp duty charges (27%)
  • Current mortgage rates (24%)
  • The price gap between properties (24%)

Income pressures continue to affect many households, with 45% of working adults stating their wages aren't keeping pace with rising living costs. This financial strain appears to be influencing buyer behaviour, with transaction data showing an increase in purchases below £500,000 and more next-time buyers putting down deposits under £20,000.

The growing cost of moving up the ladder

The research reveals stark differences in financial requirements at various stages of the property ladder. Second-steppers — those moving from their first home — estimate needing an average of £75,648 in additional funds after accounting for sale proceeds. This includes:

  • £41,751 for a deposit
  • £28,112 in stamp duty
  • £5,785 in associated costs

By contrast, those purchasing their third or subsequent home typically require less additional funding (£52,651 on average), with 43% of this group reporting no need to save for a deposit at all.

As economic uncertainty persists, homeowners may wish to review their financial plans carefully. Speaking with a mortgage adviser could help borrowers understand their options, whether considering remortgaging, overpayments or timing a property move.

item.title: Cost of renovating UK’s vacant stock rises 19% to £34bn
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals the staggering cost of bringing Britain’s empty homes back into use has surged by nearly a fifth in just twelve months. With renovation expenses now reaching £34 billion nationwide, this sharp increase could have significant implications for property investors and prospective homeowners alike.

Rising costs hit renovation projects

Analysis by specialist lender BuildLoan shows the total refurbishment bill for vacant UK properties has jumped 19% year-on-year. This dramatic rise outstrips general inflation and reflects growing material and labour costs across the construction sector. Homeowners considering renovation projects might find budgeting more challenging than anticipated, particularly for properties requiring extensive work.

The figures highlight several key factors driving costs upwards:

  • Persistent supply chain issues affecting building materials
  • Higher wages for skilled tradespeople
  • Increased energy efficiency requirements for modernised properties
  • Growing demand for renovation finance products

Opportunities amidst the challenges

While the rising costs present obstacles, the situation also creates potential opportunities for property investors and homebuyers willing to take on renovation projects. Bringing empty homes back into use could help address housing shortages in some areas, whilst potentially offering better value than purchasing newly built properties at premium prices.

Those exploring renovation options might consider:

  • Comparing specialist renovation mortgage products
  • Researching local property markets with high vacancy rates
  • Seeking professional advice on planning permissions and building regulations
  • Exploring government schemes that support property refurbishment

As renovation costs continue to climb, thorough financial planning becomes increasingly important for anyone considering bringing vacant properties back to life. With careful research and professional guidance, these projects could still represent worthwhile investments despite the challenging economic climate.

item.title: North American buyers lead overseas demand shift in UK housing market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

North American buyers are driving a notable shift in overseas demand for UK property, according to recent analysis of buyer registration data. In the first quarter of 2026, purchasers from the United States and Canada represented 19% of all international buyers in Britain – marking a four percentage point increase year-on-year and more than doubling their share over the past decade. This growth comes despite a 10% overall decline in foreign buyer interest across the UK market.

Changing international buyer trends

The latest figures reveal a clear divergence in overseas demand patterns. Whilst European buyers still dominate with 54% of international applications, their share has fallen by eight percentage points over the last ten years. Meanwhile, North American interest continues to strengthen, with registrations rising 13% annually. Market analysts suggest this reflects changing perceptions of UK property, with North American buyers increasingly viewing Britain as a place to live rather than purely an investment opportunity.

At the same time, demand from Middle Eastern buyers has weakened significantly, accounting for just 5% of overseas applications – the lowest proportion since 2013. Recent geopolitical tensions appear to have accelerated this decline, with registrations from the region falling 27% month-on-month following the outbreak of conflict.

London remains focal point for overseas buyers

Geographical patterns in international demand show considerable variation across the UK. London stands out as the only region to record growth in foreign buyer interest during Q1 2026, with registrations up 8% year-on-year. The capital now attracts 25% of all overseas enquiries, up from 21% twelve months earlier.

North American buyers in particular are showing increased interest in London properties, with 28% now focusing their search on the capital compared to 24% last year and just 17% five years ago. However, preferences within London have shifted, with only 5% of North American buyers currently targeting prime central locations – down from 13% at the 2013 peak.

Market observers note that relative value perceptions and favourable exchange rates may be contributing to these trends, particularly as London property prices remain approximately 3% below their 2022 levels. With international demand patterns continuing to evolve, the UK property market appears to be entering a new phase of global engagement.

item.title: Six ways the Renters Rights Act will change renting: Part 5, Rent Issues
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Renters Rights Act 2025 introduces significant changes to the UK rental market, particularly concerning rent-related issues. With rising rents becoming a pressing concern for tenants and policymakers alike, the new legislation aims to create a fairer and more transparent renting environment. Here’s a closer look at how the Act addresses key rent-related challenges.

Tackling Rising Rent Levels

For many years, tenants have often felt unable to challenge rent increases due to fears of eviction under Section 21 notices. This has contributed to rents rising faster than inflation in numerous areas, making housing in the UK among the most expensive compared to similar countries. The Renters Rights Act seeks to address this imbalance by introducing measures to protect tenants from unjustified rent hikes. By limiting landlords’ ability to increase rents arbitrarily, the legislation aims to ensure that rent rises are fair and proportionate.

Putting an End to Rental Bidding

One of the issues the Act aims to resolve is the practice of rental bidding, where tenants offer higher rents to secure properties amidst a shortage of suitable accommodation. To combat this, landlords will now be required to advertise a ‘proposed rent’ in property listings, which they cannot exceed. Additionally, local authorities will have the power to fine landlords who fail to comply with this requirement or allow tenants to bid above the advertised rent. This change is designed to create a more level playing field for renters and prevent inflated rental prices.

Restrictions on Rent in Advance

Another factor contributing to the high cost of renting has been landlords demanding large sums of rent in advance, particularly from tenants with less-than-ideal credit histories. The new Act prohibits landlords from accepting rent payments before the tenancy agreement is signed. Breaching this rule could result in fines of up to £5,000 per offence, enforced by Trading Standards. This measure aims to reduce the financial burden on tenants and make renting more accessible.

Changes to Rent Increase Procedures

Previously, landlords could increase rents through various methods, including new tenancy agreements, rent review clauses, or the statutory notice procedure under Section 13 of the Housing Act 1988. However, the Renters Rights Act now limits rent increases to the statutory notice procedure only. While landlords and tenants can still agree on new rent levels, these can only be implemented after a Section 13 notice has been served. This change ensures that rent increases are transparent and in line with legal requirements, with rent review clauses in tenancy agreements becoming unenforceable.

The Renters Rights Act 2025 represents a significant step towards creating a fairer rental market in the UK. By addressing issues such as rising rents, rental bidding, and upfront payments, the legislation aims to provide greater protection and stability for tenants. Homeowners and landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with these changes to ensure compliance and foster positive landlord-tenant relationships.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Renters Rights Act 2025 introduces significant changes to the UK rental market, particularly concerning rent-related issues. With rising rents becoming a pressing concern for tenants and policymakers alike, the new legislation aims to create a fairer and more transparent renting environment. Here’s a closer look at how the Act addresses key rent-related challenges.

Tackling Rising Rent Levels

For many years, tenants have often felt unable to challenge rent increases due to fears of eviction under Section 21 notices. This has contributed to rents rising faster than inflation in numerous areas, making housing in the UK among the most expensive compared to similar countries. The Renters Rights Act seeks to address this imbalance by introducing measures to protect tenants from unjustified rent hikes. By limiting landlords’ ability to increase rents arbitrarily, the legislation aims to ensure that rent rises are fair and proportionate.

Putting an End to Rental Bidding

One of the issues the Act aims to resolve is the practice of rental bidding, where tenants offer higher rents to secure properties amidst a shortage of suitable accommodation. To combat this, landlords will now be required to advertise a ‘proposed rent’ in property listings, which they cannot exceed. Additionally, local authorities will have the power to fine landlords who fail to comply with this requirement or allow tenants to bid above the advertised rent. This change is designed to create a more level playing field for renters and prevent inflated rental prices.

Restrictions on Rent in Advance

Another factor contributing to the high cost of renting has been landlords demanding large sums of rent in advance, particularly from tenants with less-than-ideal credit histories. The new Act prohibits landlords from accepting rent payments before the tenancy agreement is signed. Breaching this rule could result in fines of up to £5,000 per offence, enforced by Trading Standards. This measure aims to reduce the financial burden on tenants and make renting more accessible.

Changes to Rent Increase Procedures

Previously, landlords could increase rents through various methods, including new tenancy agreements, rent review clauses, or the statutory notice procedure under Section 13 of the Housing Act 1988. However, the Renters Rights Act now limits rent increases to the statutory notice procedure only. While landlords and tenants can still agree on new rent levels, these can only be implemented after a Section 13 notice has been served. This change ensures that rent increases are transparent and in line with legal requirements, with rent review clauses in tenancy agreements becoming unenforceable.

The Renters Rights Act 2025 represents a significant step towards creating a fairer rental market in the UK. By addressing issues such as rising rents, rental bidding, and upfront payments, the legislation aims to provide greater protection and stability for tenants. Homeowners and landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with these changes to ensure compliance and foster positive landlord-tenant relationships.

item.title: Liz Truss and Russell Quirk break down the housing crisis – and who’s to blame
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing crisis remains a pressing issue, with affordability pressures continuing to dominate the market. In a recent discussion, former Prime Minister Liz Truss and property commentator Russell Quirk explored the key drivers behind these challenges, shedding light on the role of government policies, market dynamics, and regulatory changes.

Policy Changes and Their Impact

One of the central themes of the conversation was the effect of recent and proposed policy measures on the housing market. Quirk highlighted how initiatives such as the Renters’ Reform Bill and Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) requirements are influencing landlord behaviour. He argued that these changes are prompting many landlords to exit the private rented sector, reducing the availability of rental properties and driving up rents.

The discussion also touched on the broader impact of government intervention, including the Help to Buy scheme. While this initiative aimed to stimulate demand, critics argue that it has done little to address supply constraints, exacerbating affordability issues for younger buyers. Quirk emphasised that both Labour and Conservative policies have contributed to worsening market conditions over the years.

Monetary Policy and Market Dynamics

The Bank of England’s monetary policy was another focal point. Truss and Quirk explored how low interest rates and quantitative easing have influenced asset price inflation, including house prices. They discussed the challenges this creates for first-time buyers, particularly in an environment where wage growth has not kept pace with rising property values.

Immigration and its effect on housing demand also featured in the conversation. Quirk noted that increased population growth, driven by immigration, has put additional pressure on an already strained housing supply. This, combined with perceived shortcomings in housing delivery by successive governments, has left many feeling the system is failing to meet the needs of the population.

The Role of Planning and Long-Term Solutions

The pair also examined the influence of local planning decisions on housing supply. Quirk argued that bureaucratic hurdles and resistance to new developments are significant barriers to addressing the crisis. He suggested that housing policy is often shaped by short-term political considerations rather than long-term solutions, leaving the market in a state of perpetual imbalance.

The discussion concluded with a reflection on the upcoming local elections and their potential to serve as a barometer of public sentiment towards housing policy. Both Truss and Quirk agreed that politicians have historically struggled to deliver effective solutions to the housing crisis, leaving homeowners and renters alike facing ongoing challenges.

As the debate around housing affordability continues, it remains clear that a multifaceted approach will be needed to address the root causes of the crisis. From regulatory reforms to strategic planning decisions, stakeholders across the board will need to work together to create a more balanced and sustainable housing market.

item.title: Is the great British love affair with property finally on the rocks?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For decades, property ownership has been a cornerstone of British financial aspirations, with many viewing bricks and mortar as a reliable long-term investment. However, recent regulatory changes and market pressures have led some to question whether the UK’s property landscape is becoming increasingly challenging for both landlords and homeowners alike.

The shifting landscape for buy-to-let investors

The private rental sector has undergone significant transformation in recent years, with a wave of new regulations altering the dynamics for landlords. The abolition of Section 21 ‘no-fault’ evictions, part of the Renters’ Reform Bill, has been particularly contentious. Whilst designed to protect tenants, some property investors worry it may complicate legitimate property management decisions, such as selling or refurbishing.

Additional pressures on landlords include:

  • Tax changes, including reduced mortgage interest relief and higher stamp duty surcharges
  • Increasing energy efficiency requirements, with EPC targets moving towards band C
  • Expanded safety regulations covering everything from electrical checks to fire safety standards
  • Local licensing schemes that vary significantly between authorities

Broader challenges in the housing market

Beyond the rental sector, homeowners face their own set of hurdles. Rising interest rates have increased mortgage costs substantially, whilst house price growth has slowed in many areas. First-time buyers in particular may find affordability remains stretched, despite some recent price corrections.

Those considering property investment might wish to weigh several factors:

  • The potential for ongoing regulatory changes affecting both rental and ownership markets
  • The impact of higher borrowing costs on investment returns
  • Alternative investment options that may offer different risk/reward profiles
  • The time commitment required for property management versus more passive investments

While property remains an important part of many Britons’ financial plans, the current climate suggests careful research and professional advice could be valuable when making housing-related decisions. The market continues to evolve, and what worked in previous decades may not necessarily deliver the same results going forward.

item.title: Major conveyancing firm enters administration after sale collapse
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

BLB Solicitors, a prominent conveyancing firm based in Trowbridge, has entered administration following the collapse of a proposed sale. The firm ceased trading as of midday on Thursday, leaving clients in the midst of property transactions potentially affected. BLB, which operates across six offices in Bath, Bristol, Bradford-on-Avon, Swindon, Almondsbury, and Trowbridge, employs over 40 lawyers and has been a key player in the region’s property market.

Impact on Clients

BLB Solicitors has advised clients to seek alternative legal representation to ensure their property transactions continue without disruption. In a letter sent to clients, the firm acknowledged the inconvenience caused and apologised for the additional stress this situation may create. The letter also reassured clients that their files and documents are being prepared for a swift transfer to another solicitor.

Homeowners currently in the process of buying or selling property may wish to act promptly to minimise delays. Those affected are encouraged to contact their mortgage lenders or estate agents for guidance on how to proceed. It could also be worth reaching out to other conveyancing firms to secure their services without delay.

Administration Process

The firm’s closure follows the withdrawal of an anticipated sale at short notice, which led to the appointment of administrators. According to Companies House filings, five directors left BLB Solicitors between 30 March and 1 April, signalling internal upheaval prior to the administration. The insolvency notice was officially published in The London Gazette on 13 April, confirming the formal administration process.

BLB Solicitors has stated that it is engaging with other interested parties, and if a sale is agreed upon, clients will be updated immediately. However, until such a resolution is reached, borrowers and property buyers may need to take proactive steps to safeguard their transactions.

Next Steps for Affected Parties

For those impacted by BLB Solicitors’ closure, there are several practical steps to consider. Firstly, instructing a new conveyancing firm as soon as possible could help avoid unnecessary delays. Secondly, keeping open lines of communication with estate agents, mortgage brokers, and lenders may ensure all parties are aligned during the transition.

Additionally, homeowners might wish to request copies of their transaction documents from BLB Solicitors to facilitate a smoother handover to a new solicitor. Whilst the situation is undoubtedly challenging, taking these steps could help mitigate the impact on property transactions.

The sudden closure of BLB Solicitors highlights the importance of staying informed and proactive during property transactions. By acting swiftly, affected clients can work towards minimising disruption and moving forward with their plans.

item.title: Buy-to-let retreat continues as over 250,000 former rented homes come to market
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Recent analysis by Savills reveals a notable trend in the UK property market, with a growing number of buy-to-let landlords choosing to sell their rental properties. Over the 12 months leading up to March, approximately 254,000 former rental homes were listed for sale, equating to nearly 700 properties per day. This marks a significant 28% increase compared to the previous year, highlighting a shift in landlord behaviour amid evolving market conditions.

What’s Driving the Surge in Sales?

The rise in landlords exiting the buy-to-let market can be attributed to several factors. The upcoming Renters’ Rights Act has prompted many to reassess their investments, whilst the end of fixed-rate mortgage deals has added financial pressure. Additionally, stricter regulations, such as higher energy efficiency standards, have made it more challenging for smaller landlords to maintain profitability. These combined pressures have led many to question whether rental properties remain a viable investment.

London has been particularly affected, with former rental properties accounting for 30% of new sales instructions, compared to 13% in the rest of Great Britain. This regional disparity underscores the unique challenges faced by landlords in the capital, where higher property values and regulatory costs may be driving the trend.

What Happens to These Properties?

While many of these homes are being sold, not all are leaving the rental market entirely. Savills found that 14% of properties sold were purchased by other landlords, effectively returning to the private rented sector. This suggests a broader restructuring of the market, with a shift towards a smaller, more professional pool of landlords.

Looking ahead, factors such as refinancing needs and tenants choosing to move on are likely to continue driving sales. However, the market is not simply shrinking; it is evolving, with a focus on sustainability and long-term commitment. As the landscape changes, homeowners and investors may wish to carefully consider their options and seek professional advice to navigate these shifts effectively.

The buy-to-let market is clearly undergoing significant transformation, with landlords facing a range of challenges that are reshaping the sector. Whether this trend continues will depend on broader economic conditions and regulatory developments, making it an area to watch closely in the coming months.
